.ddx/attachments/ddx-ebf3c09b/events.jsonl:3:{"actor":"erik","body":"Bead ddx-ebf3c09b — repo-wide verb migration + agent_*.go cleanup\n===================================================================\n\nNO CHANGES committed in this attempt.\n\n## Why this bead is currently blocked\n\nThe bead description has four parts. Three of the four are blocked on a\nprerequisite that has not landed:\n\n(a) \"Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands\"\n    (agent_list/capabilities/doctor/check/models/providers/route_status/\n    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay)\n\n(c) \"Register DDx overrides on mounted subtree (deprecations + condense)\"\n\nBoth presuppose a mounted upstream Cobra root grafted under `ddx agent`\n— i.e. `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))` per FEAT-001-cli.md:69 and\nplan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md:28.\n\nThe CONTRACT-003 audit (committed 08a40b69) explicitly identifies this\nas a gap (docs/helix/02-design/contract-003-audit-2026-04-29.md:23–89):\n\n    > DDx cannot write `agentcli.NewRootCommand()` against the current\n    > contract.\n    >\n    > The plan's \"delete with prejudice\" list (agent_list,\n    > agent_capabilities, agent_doctor, agent_check, agent_models,\n    > agent_providers, agent_route_status, agent_catalog,\n    > agent_reindex) presupposes that upstream owns these subcommands\n    > inside a mountable tree. Today they exist only as flag-driven\n    > branches in cmd/agent/main.go that DDx cannot consume\n    > programmatically.\n    >\n    > Plan bead #6 (\"open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent`\n    > if #5 surfaces gaps\") is **required, not conditional**, because\n    > of this gap.\n\nConcretely:\n\n  * cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (lines 123–141) wires every soon-to-be-deleted\n    file via `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgentCheckCommand())`,\n    `newAgentModelsCommand()`, `newAgentProvidersCommand()`,\n    `newAgentRouteStatusCommand()`, `newAgentListCommand()`,\n    `newAgentCapabilitiesCommand()`, `newAgentDoctorCommand()`,\n    `newAgentBenchmarkCommand()`, `newAgentCatalogCommand()`,\n    `newAgentReplayCommand()`. Deleting any of those `agent_*.go`\n    files without the upstream `MountCLI` replacement would leave\n    agent_cmd.go referencing missing constructors and break the build.\n  * The replacement (`cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`) requires\n    the upstream module to expose `MountCLI` from a non-`internal/`\n    package, which the audit specifies as the upstream amendment\n    CONTRACT-003-A (port `cmd/agent/main.go` from `flag` to `cobra`,\n    move into `agent/cli`, re-export `MountCLI`, bump module version).\n    That work has not happened in `~/Projects/agent`.\n\n(b) \"Migrate DDx-only subcommands\"\n    (agent_executions → tries_*; agent_log → runs_log;\n     agent_metrics → runs_metrics; agent_usage folded into runs_metrics;\n     agent_workers → work_workers)\n\nThis portion is mechanically possible inside DDx alone, but per the\nplan (line 208) beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — with #15\nlanding in isolation, the new namespaces (`ddx runs`, `ddx tries`,\n`ddx work workers`) need top-level registration that has no analogue\nyet in command_factory_commands.go. The implementation P2 phase of the\nplan (lines 196–204) lists \"CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work\nworkers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)\" as separate work\nthat comes after the frame/design beads. Doing only (b) here, without\n(a) and (c), produces a confusing CLI surface where the same evidence\nis reachable both as `ddx agent executions` and `ddx tries list`, and\nit strands the deprecation messages that need to point at the new\nverbs.\n\n(d) \"Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to\n    new verbs\"\n\nThis depends on the new verbs (`ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx runs *`,\n`ddx tries *`, `ddx work workers *`) actually existing as runnable\ncommands so that the updated scripts/skills run green. `ddx run` and\n`ddx try` are not registered today (grep for `Use:.*\\\"run\\\"` /\n`Use:.*\\\"try\\\"` against cli/cmd/*.go shows only the `ddx agent run`\ninner command). Updating scripts/eval-skill.sh,\nscripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh, scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh,\nscripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,\nlibrary/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md, etc. to call\nnon-existent verbs would break those flows immediately and would\nspecifically break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced\nthis very plan (plan line 208).\n\n## What an unblocked attempt needs\n\nRoughly in dependency order:\n\n1. Upstream amendment CONTRACT-003-A merged in `~/Projects/agent`:\n   port to cobra, move commands to `agent/cli`, export `MountCLI`,\n   tag a release, and bump `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` in\n   DDx's go.mod (no replace directive — see memory\n   feedback_no_local_replace).\n2. Top-level `ddx run` and `ddx try` registered as first-class\n   commands under CommandFactory (the implementation P2 thread of the\n   plan).\n3. `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` namespace skeletons\n   registered, even if their subcommand implementations are then\n   ported in this bead.\n\nOnce those are in, the present bead becomes:\n\n  * Replace the 11+ enumerated `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgent*Command())`\n    calls in agent_cmd.go with `cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`,\n    plus deprecation override registrations for `agent run`,\n    `agent execute-bead`, `agent execute-loop`, and the override for\n    `agent condense` (DDx-specific filter).\n  * `git rm` agent_check.go, agent_models.go, agent_providers.go,\n    agent_route_status.go (the only \"delete with prejudice\" files\n    that actually exist in DDx today; agent_list/capabilities/doctor/\n    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay only exist as inline functions\n    inside agent_cmd.go and would be removed by the agent_cmd.go\n    rewrite).\n  * Move agent_executions.go → tries_*.go; agent_metrics.go →\n    runs_metrics.go (folding agent_usage.go into it);\n    agent_workers.go → work_workers.go; rename `newAgentLogCommand`\n    in agent_cmd.go into `runs_log.go`. Update tests in lockstep.\n  * Sweep scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to\n    swap old verbs for new (e.g. `ddx agent run` → `ddx run`,\n    `ddx agent execute-loop` → `ddx work`,\n    `ddx agent execute-bead` → `ddx try`,\n    `ddx agent log/metrics/usage/executions/workers` → the new\n    `runs/tries/work workers` paths). Specific known references:\n    scripts/eval-skill.sh, scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh,\n    scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh, scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,\n    library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md,\n    library/personas/README.md, cli/cmd/init.go:757,\n    cli/cmd/bead_review.go:30–33, plus a wider grep across\n    docs/**/*.md and library/**/*.md.\n\n## What is done in this attempt\n\nNothing on disk — but the verification above (audit cross-reference,\nagent_cmd.go wiring inspection, FEAT-001 surface inspection,\nsearch for top-level `run`/`try` registrations) is captured here so\nthe next attempt can move directly into execution once bead #6 lands.\n\n## Recommended next steps\n\n- Park this bead behind bead #6 (upstream CONTRACT-003-A amendment)\n  and the P2 implementation beads that introduce top-level\n  `ddx run` / `ddx try` and the `runs`/`tries`/`work workers`\n  namespace skeletons.\n- Re-attempt with the same id once those prerequisites have merged.\n  At that point this bead is mechanical: a contained refactor of\n  agent_cmd.go plus a directed grep-and-edit sweep of the call sites\n  enumerated above.\nrationale: Bead ddx-ebf3c09b — repo-wide verb migration + agent_*.go cleanup\n===================================================================\n\nNO CHANGES committed in this attempt.\n\n## Why this bead is currently blocked\n\nThe bead description has four parts. Three of the four are blocked on a\nprerequisite that has not landed:\n\n(a) \"Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands\"\n    (agent_list/capabilities/doctor/check/models/providers/route_status/\n    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay)\n\n(c) \"Register DDx overrides on mounted subtree (deprecations + condense)\"\n\nBoth presuppose a mounted upstream Cobra root grafted under `ddx agent`\n— i.e. `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))` per FEAT-001-cli.md:69 and\nplan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md:28.\n\nThe CONTRACT-003 audit (committed 08a40b69) explicitly identifies this\nas a gap (docs/helix/02-design/contract-003-audit-2026-04-29.md:23–89):\n\n    > DDx cannot write `agentcli.NewRootCommand()` against the current\n    > contract.\n    >\n    > The plan's \"delete with prejudice\" list (agent_list,\n    > agent_capabilities, agent_doctor, agent_check, agent_models,\n    > agent_providers, agent_route_status, agent_catalog,\n    > agent_reindex) presupposes that upstream owns these subcommands\n    > inside a mountable tree. Deleting any of those `agent_*.go`\n    files without the upstream `MountCLI` replacement would leave\n    agent_cmd.go referencing missing constructors and break the build.\n  * The replacement (`cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`) requires\n    the upstream module to expose `MountCLI` from a non-`internal/`\n    package, which the audit specifies as the upstream amendment\n    CONTRACT-003-A (port `cmd/agent/main.go` from `flag` to `cobra`,\n    move into `agent/cli`, re-export `MountCLI`, bump module version).\n    That work has not happened in `~/Projects/agent`.\n\n(b) \"Migrate DDx-only subcommands\"\n    (agent_executions → tries_*; agent_log → runs_log;\n     agent_metrics → runs_metrics; agent_usage folded into runs_metrics;\n     agent_workers → work_workers)\n\nThis portion is mechanically possible inside DDx alone, but per the\nplan (line 208) beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — with #15\nlanding in isolation, the new namespaces (`ddx runs`, `ddx tries`,\n`ddx work workers`) need top-level registration that has no analogue\nyet in command_factory_commands.go. The implementation P2 phase of the\nplan (lines 196–204) lists \"CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work\nworkers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)\" as separate work\nthat comes after the frame/design beads. Doing only (b) here, without\n(a) and (c), produces a confusing CLI surface where the same evidence\nis reachable both as `ddx agent executions` and `ddx tries list`, and\nit strands the deprecation messages that need to point at the new\nverbs.\n\n(d) \"Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to\n    new verbs\"\n\nThis depends on the new verbs (`ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx runs *`,\n`ddx tries *`, `ddx work workers *`) actually existing as runnable\ncommands so that the updated scripts/skills run green. `ddx run` and\n`ddx try` are not registered today (grep for `Use:.*\\\"run\\\"` /\n`Use:.*\\\"try\\\"` against cli/cmd/*.go shows only the `ddx agent run`\ninner command). Updating scripts/eval-skill.sh,\nscripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh, scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh,\nscripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,\nlibrary/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md, etc. to call\nnon-existent verbs would break those flows immediately and would\nspecifically break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced\nthis very plan (plan line 208).\n\n## What an unblocked attempt needs\n\nRoughly in dependency order:\n\n1. Upstream amendment CONTRACT-003-A merged in `~/Projects/agent`:\n   port to cobra, move commands to `agent/cli`, export `MountCLI`,\n   tag a release, and bump `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` in\n   DDx's go.mod (no replace directive — see memory\n   feedback_no_local_replace).\n2. Top-level `ddx run` and `ddx try` registered as first-class\n   commands under CommandFactory (the implementation P2 thread of the\n   plan).\n3. `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` namespace skeletons\n   registered, even if their subcommand implementations are then\n   ported in this bead.\n\nOnce those are in, the present bead becomes:\n\n  * Replace the 11+ enumerated `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgent*Command())`\n    calls in agent_cmd.go with `cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`,\n    plus deprecation override registrations for `agent run`,\n    `agent execute-bead`, `agent execute-loop`, and the override for\n    `agent condense` (DDx-specific filter).\n  * `git rm` agent_check.go, agent_models.go, agent_providers.go,\n    agent_route_status.go (the only \"delete with prejudice\" files\n    that actually exist in DDx today; agent_list/capabilities/doctor/\n    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay only exist as inline functions\n    inside agent_cmd.go and would be removed by the agent_cmd.go\n    rewrite).\n  * Move agent_executions.go → tries_*.go; agent_metrics.go →\n    runs_metrics.go (folding agent_usage.go into it);\n    agent_workers.go → work_workers.go; rename `newAgentLogCommand`\n    in agent_cmd.go into `runs_log.go`. Update tests in lockstep.\n  * Sweep scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to\n    swap old verbs for new (e.g. `ddx agent run` → `ddx run`,\n    `ddx agent execute-loop` → `ddx work`,\n    `ddx agent execute-bead` → `ddx try`,\n    `ddx agent log/metrics/usage/executions/workers` → the new\n    `runs/tries/work workers` paths). Specific known references:\n    scripts/eval-skill.sh, scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh,\n    scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh, scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,\n    library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md,\n    library/personas/README.md, cli/cmd/init.go:757,\n    cli/cmd/bead_review.go:30–33, plus a wider grep across\n    docs/**/*.md and library/**/*.md.\n\n## What is done in this attempt\n\nNothing on disk — but the verification above (audit cross-reference,\nagent_cmd.go wiring inspection, FEAT-001 surface inspection,\nsearch for top-level `run`/`try` registrations) is captured here so\nthe next attempt can move directly into execution once bead #6 lands.\n\n## Recommended next steps\n\n- Park this bead behind bead #6 (upstream CONTRACT-003-A amendment)\n  and the P2 implementation beads that introduce top-level\n  `ddx run` / `ddx try` and the `runs`/`tries`/`work workers`\n  namespace skeletons.\n- Re-attempt with the same id once those prerequisites have merged.\n  At that point this bead is mechanical: a contained refactor of\n  agent_cmd.go plus a directed grep-and-edit sweep of the call sites\n  enumerated above.\nresult_rev=a2d7da5e8a5ef09edfde6bb416eb9fd0a018d818\nbase_rev=a2d7da5e8a5ef09edfde6bb416eb9fd0a018d818","created_at":"2026-04-30T00:47:01.771728126Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"already_satisfied"}

They are the raw CLI inputs. The gap is not here\\n— it is at the normalization boundary (Layer 2).\\n\\n## Layer 2 — Normalize flags into `RouteRequest`\\n\\nThe normalization boundary converts raw CLI flags into the minimal routing ask\\nthat the routing engine evaluates.\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:289` — `NormalizeRouteRequest(flags RouteFlags, cfg Config, catalog *Catalog) RouteRequest`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:294` — `RouteRequest{Effort, Permissions}`\\n  initial struct. Note the absence of `Provider`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:303` — `flags.Harness != \\\"\\\"` branch assigns\\n  `HarnessOverride` directly.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305` — `flags.Provider != \\\"\\\"` branch: the\\n  provider flag is **only** consulted as a harness-alias lookup. If\\n  `--provider vidar-omlx` does not match any entry in `builtinHarnesses`, the\\n  value is silently discarded.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:318` — `flags.Model != \\\"\\\"` branch routes the\\n  model through `catalog.NormalizeModelRef` to produce either `ModelRef` (known\\n  in catalog) or `ModelPin` (exact, bypasses catalog policy).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/types.go:337` — `RouteRequest` struct definition. Fields:\\n  `Profile`, `ModelRef`, `ModelPin`, `Effort`, `Permissions`, `HarnessOverride`.\\n  **No `Provider` field — see Gap #1.**\\n\\n**Concrete outcome for our invocation:**\\n`--provider vidar-omlx` does not name a registered harness, so the provider\\nflag is dropped at `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305`. `--model-ref qwen/qwen3.6` flows through\\n`NormalizeModelRef`; since it is not in the shared catalog, it becomes a\\n`ModelPin`. The resulting `RouteRequest` carries only\\n`{ModelPin: \\\"qwen/qwen3.6\\\"}`.\\n\\n## Layer 3 — Discovery probe\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:117` — `init()` sets the process-wide cache\\n  TTL to `30 * time.Second`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:124` — `DiscoverProviderModels(workDir, timeout)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:125` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:130` — cache hit returns the cached result if still fresh.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:141` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:155` — probes every configured provider via `providerstatus.Probe`; silently skips unreachable ones (partial failure is tolerated).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:162` — cache write under mutex.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:170` — `InvalidateDiscoveryCache` is the only\\n  way to force a re-probe in tests.\\n\\nDiscovery is invoked on the routing hot-path only when a `ModelPin` is not in\\nthe catalog:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:576` — in `ProbeAndBuildCandidatePlans`, the\\n  discovery probe fires when `req.ModelPin != \\\"\\\" && req.ModelRef == \\\"\\\" && r.Discovery == nil`.\\n\\nDiscovery is invoked a second time on the dispatch path:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:521` — in `resolveNativeAgentProvider`,\\n  `r.Discovery.ProvidersForModel(model)` looks up which providers advertised\\n  the requested model.\\n\\n## Layer 4 — Candidate planning\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:27` — `BuildCandidatePlans(req, stateOverride)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:33` through `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:46` — iterates every registered harness. `TestOnly` harnesses (script, virtual) are skipped unless `req.HarnessOverride` names them explicitly — this is the guard that prevents `script` from leaking into standard-tier fallback chains (see ddx-869848ec in the comment on line 40).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:47` — calls `evaluateCandidate` per harness.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:106` — `evaluateCandidate(name, harness, req, stateOverride)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:396` — `rejectIfNotViable(plan, req)` runs the\\n  viability gate. Checks:\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:399` — `!s.Installed`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:402` — `!s.Reachable`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:405` — `!s.Authenticated`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:408` — quota exhausted.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:411` — degraded.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:414` — policy violation.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:418` — harness override mismatch.\\n\\nNote: none of these check *capability* — context window, tool support, effort\\nlevel support. That is Gap #6.\\n\\n## Layer 5 — Fuzzy matching\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:66` — `FuzzyMatchModel(input)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:70` — delegates to `fuzzyMatchInPool` using\\n  `d.AllModels()` (a flat deduplicated pool across all providers — not per-provider).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:76` — `fuzzyMatchInPool(input, pool)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:78` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:82` — exact-match short-circuit.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:89` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:96` — prefix match: candidate `m` is considered if `len(m) > len(input) && m[:len(input)] == input`. **The comparison is case-sensitive and treats slashes literally (Gap #3).**\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:100` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:106` — tiebreak: shortest suffix wins; alphabetical on equal suffix length. **Not version-aware (Gap #5).**\\n\\n**Concrete outcome for our invocation:**\\nPool likely contains `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit`. Input `qwen/qwen3.6` fails the\\nexact check, then fails the prefix check because `Q != q` and the slash is not\\nin the pool entries. The function returns `(\\\"\\\", false)`. `resolveNativeAgentProvider`\\nfalls through to the `isOpenRouterModel` heuristic at\\n`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:544` (vendor/model format → openrouter),\\nwhich is not what the operator wanted.\\n\\n## Layer 6 — Scoring\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:477` — `scoreCandidate(profile, plan)` entry.\\n  Returns a `float64`; higher is better.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:478` — base score `100`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:485` — `switch profile` over `cheap`, `standard`, `smart`, default.\\n  - `cheap` (lines 486–493): `local` +40, within-quota subscription +20, penalize `cr * 30`.\\n  - `standard` (lines 495–502): `local` +25, within-quota +15, penalize `cr * 10`.\\n  - `smart` (lines 504–512): `cr * 20` bonus (expensive > cheap), within-quota +5.\\n  - default (lines 514–522): treated as standard.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:525` — quota >80%: penalty scales with over-usage.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:528` — quota state unknown: −3.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:531` — routing-signal freshness: `cached` −1, `stale` −4.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:542` — historical success rate (≥3 samples):\\n  ≥0.8 +20, <0.5 −30.\\n\\n**No provider-affinity bonus (Gap #4).** If two harnesses both report viable\\nfor the same requested model, `scoreCandidate` does not look at\\n`plan.Provider` vs the provider the model was discovered on.\\n\\n## Layer 7 — Candidate ranking and selection\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:434` — `RankCandidates(profile, plans)`.\\n  - Assigns scores (line 444) or `-1` for non-viable (line 441).\\n  - Stable sort (lines 448–466): viable before non-viable; descending score;\\n    tiebreak prefers local-cost candidates, then alphabetical harness name.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:556` — `SelectBestCandidate(plans)` returns\\n  the first viable plan, or `\\\"no viable harness candidate: all harnesses rejected\\\"`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:567` — `ProbeAndBuildCandidatePlans` is the\\n  full-probe wrapper used by actual dispatch (it calls live `ProbeHarnessState`\\n  per harness before building plans).\\n\\n## Layer 8 — Dispatch\\n\\n`Runner.Run` is the dispatch fork:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:141` — `Run(opts RunOptions) (*Result, error)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:148` — `resolveHarness(opts)` picks the harness.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:376` — `resolveHarness(opts)` definition.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:382` — **Gap #2 origin**: if `opts.Harness == \\\"\\\"` and\\n  `opts.Provider != \\\"\\\"`, the `--provider` value is looked up as a harness\\n  alias. If it matches a registered harness (e.g. `agent`, `local`), that\\n  harness is chosen; otherwise `resolveHarness` falls back to `Config.Harness`.\\n  This is a parallel path that bypasses `NormalizeRouteRequest` entirely.\\n\\nDispatch branches (in order):\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:165` — `harnessName == \\\"virtual\\\"` → `RunVirtual(opts)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:170` — `harnessName == \\\"agent\\\"` → `RunAgent(opts)` (embedded).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:175` — `harnessName == \\\"script\\\"` → `RunScript(opts)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:187` — HTTP-provider harnesses with no binary (`lmstudio`, `openrouter`): surfaces the harness name as `opts.Provider` and delegates to `RunAgent`. The comment on lines 185–186 references `ddx-501e87ef` — the bug where cheap-tier attempts were burning <1s with \\\"exec: no command\\\".\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:278` — default exec path: `Executor.ExecuteInDir(ctx, harness.Binary, args, stdin, execDir)`.\\n\\nEmbedded agent dispatch:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:65` — `RunAgent(opts)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:94` — `resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider(wd, model, opts.Provider, opts.ModelRef)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:464` — `resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:493` — `resolveNativeAgentProvider(workDir, model, explicitProvider, explicitModelRef)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:519` — **Gap #2 again**: `explicitProvider != \\\"\\\"` overrides the default provider with the raw `opts.Provider` string. This is where `--provider vidar-omlx` finally re-enters the flow, but by now the routing engine has already ranked candidates without knowing about it.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:521` through `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:548` — discovery-based provider resolution: if the model isn't in the catalog and discovery has results, route to the first provider that advertises the model (exact or fuzzy).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:555` — `cfg.BuildProviderWithOverrides(providerName, overrides)` materializes the provider object from the native agent config.\\n\\n## Layer 9 — Wire-level HTTP request\\n\\nThe embedded agent library builds the request body and dispatches to the\\nprovider endpoint. DDx hands off at `agentlib.Run`.\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:127` — system prompt construction via\\n  `prompt.NewFromPreset(agentCfg.Preset).WithTools(tools).WithContextFiles(...).WithWorkDir(wd).Build()`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:153` — `agentlib.Request` assembly:\\n  `Prompt`, `SystemPrompt`, `Provider`, `Tools`, `MaxIterations`, `WorkDir`,\\n  `Metadata`, `Compactor`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:320` — `agentResult, err := agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`\\n  — handoff into the `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` library. DDx does not\\n  own the HTTP transport from here on.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:688` — `buildAgentProvider(cfg)` creates\\n  the provider used by the request. Branches:\\n  - `openai-compat` or `openai` (line 691): `oai.New(oai.Config{BaseURL, APIKey, Model})`.\\n  - `anthropic` (line 697): `anthropic.New(anthropic.Config{APIKey, Model})`.\\n  - `virtual` (line 702): `virtual.New(virtual.Config{DictDir})`.\\n\\nFor `vidar-omlx`, the native-config resolution at `resolveNativeAgentProvider`\\nloads the provider block from `~/.config/agent/config.yaml` or\\n`.agent/config.yaml`. .ddx/attachments/ddx-6ff597ca/events.jsonl:14:{"actor":"ddx","body":"post-merge review: REQUEST_CHANGES\n**AC 7 — command path**: The command is registered as `ddx doc audit` (`cli/cmd/doc.go:59`), but the acceptance criteria specifies `ddx docs audit` (or `ddx doctor docs`). If `doc` is the existing noun, this is arguably fine, but the AC should be reconciled. The `Aliases: []string{\"integrity\"}` alias doesn't cover the spec'd names.\n**AC 7 — `--json` exit code**: `cli/cmd/doc.go:79-83` — when `--json` is set, the command encodes issues and returns `nil` unconditionally. The AC requires exit 1 when issues are present regardless of output format, so CI pipelines using `ddx doc audit --json` would incorrectly report success on a broken graph. The test `TestDocAuditCommand_JSONOutput` even documents this as intentional (\"--json always succeeds (exit 0) so piping into jq works\"), which contradicts the AC. Either the AC should be amended or the `--json` path should also return `ExitError` when issues exist (with a `--exit-zero` escape hatch for pipe-friendly use).\nresult_rev=61a8029103abff2e64442ff7b81ab92d04d84f69\nbase_rev=48f9750c2e18e96af665670c653a8f4f72e27ebf","created_at":"2026-04-23T02:38:22.039899928Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"review_request_changes"}

Updated\\n    ddx-b808df39 notes to cross-reference this investigation so the\\n    operator stop/kill tool tracks the underlying fixes.\\n\\ndiff --git a/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md b/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md\\nnew file mode 100644\\nindex 00000000..5e8547cf\\n--- /dev/null\\n+++ b/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md\\n@@ -0,0 +1,193 @@\\n+# Investigation: root cause of 33–142h hung execute-bead workers\\n+\\n+**Bead:** ddx-0a651925 (investigates; sibling to ddx-b808df39)\\n+**Attempt:** 20260418T043148-1346e8a3\\n+**Scope:** Source-code + execution-bundle analysis. No live hung worker was available\\n+in this isolated worktree to capture live `ps`/`pprof`, so the incident evidence is\\n+drawn from `.ddx/executions/*` bundles and the worker/runner source path.\\n+\\n+---\\n+\\n+## 1. Observed evidence (from `.ddx/executions/`)\\n+\\n+| Metric                                               | Count / value                                        |\\n+| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |\\n+| Total attempt bundles                                | 328                                                  |\\n+| Bundles with `result.json`                           | 291 (89%)                                            |\\n+| Bundles with `manifest.json` but **no** `result.json` | 37 (11%) — executions whose worker never finalized  |\\n+\\n+Top five longest *finalized* attempts:\\n+\\n+| `duration_ms` | Wall time    | `detail`                                                                    | Bundle                          |\\n+| ------------- | ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |\\n+| 7,720,908     | **2h 8m**    | `agent: compaction stuck: 50 consecutive failed compaction attempts`        | 20260416T230316-efbf91c5        |\\n+| 7,200,098     | **2h 0m**    | `timeout after 2h0m0s` (claude streaming wall-clock fired)                  | 20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba        |\\n+| 6,764,927     | **1h 53m**   | success (agent harness, qwen)                                               | 20260416T230315-f9f46822        |\\n+| 5,649,987     | **1h 34m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260416T230313-e193942b        |\\n+| 4,284,347     | **1h 11m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260415T103422-bb3a45fa        |\\n+\\n+**Key observation:** the 33h / 142h hang durations cited in ddx-b808df39 are\\n+~15× and ~65× longer than the longest *finalized* attempt on record. This means\\n+the hung workers never reached the point of writing `result.json` — they were\\n+blocked inside `runner.Run` (or deeper) for days. Every code path that\\n+*should* have terminated them (idle timeout, compaction-stuck, stall detector,\\n+cancel propagation) failed to fire.\\n+\\n+The 37 manifest-only bundles are candidates for hung or externally-killed\\n+workers; e.g. `20260416T215953-6b515c05` has a manifest from 2026-04-16T21:59Z\\n+with no result. Those fit the profile of \\\"workers running 33h–142h with no bead\\n+assigned\\\" reported on agent projects.\\n+\\n+---\\n+\\n+## 2. Root-cause classification (source code evidence)\\n+\\n+The investigation checked the four hypotheses from the bead description.\\n+\\n+### RC1 (PRIMARY) — Caller context is discarded inside the Runner\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (`Runner.Run`):\\n+```go\\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\\n+```\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (`Runner.RunAgent`):\\n+```go\\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\\n+```\\n+\\n+`RunOptions` (types.go) takes no `context.Context` at all. `agent.ExecuteBead`\\n+likewise does not accept ctx, and `server.WorkerManager.runWorker` passes its\\n+own ctx into `worker.Run(ctx, …)` but that ctx **never reaches the agent\\n+invocation**.\\n+\\n+**Impact:** `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (workers.go:648) calls `handle.cancel()`\\n+which cancels only the worker goroutine's ctx. The running agent provider call\\n+/ subprocess is detached. The only way to interrupt a stuck agent today is a\\n+SIGKILL from outside the process. This is the structural defect that enables\\n+33–142h hangs — once the agent is stuck, nothing inside DDx can stop it.\\n+\\n+### RC2 — Idle-reset timer, not wall-clock deadline\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/executor.go:35-40` creates an *idle timeout* context value:\\n+```go\\n+func withExecutionTimeout(ctx context.Context, timeout time.Duration) context.Context {\\n+    return context.WithValue(ctx, executionTimeoutKey{}, timeout)\\n+}\\n+```\\n+\\n+`executor.go:154-178` consumes it and **resets the timer on every stdout/stderr\\n+byte**. `agent_runner.go:264-285` implements the same pattern against agentlib\\n+events.\\n+\\n+The default timeout is 2h (`types.go:363 DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000`). The\\n+comment claims \\\"2 hours — long enough for any task; supervisor/agent decides\\n+termination\\\" but the actual behaviour is **\\\"2 hours of silence required to fire\\\"**.\\n+A provider that emits a heartbeat, a ping, a retry line, or any byte resets the\\n+timer indefinitely.\\n+\\n+The `runClaudeStreaming` path (`claude_stream.go:373-384`) is the only path that\\n+uses a wall-clock deadline — and it is the only path we see actually hitting\\n+the timeout (bundle 20260415T015231 exactly 2h 0m 0.098s).\\n+\\n+**Impact:** for `harness=agent` (qwen, minimax, openrouter, etc.), no wall-clock\\n+bound exists. 33–142h hangs are consistent with a provider that dribbles events\\n+slowly enough to defeat the idle reset.\\n+\\n+### RC3 — Compaction-stuck breaker fires late\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` increments a counter on each no-op\\n+compaction event and trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures`\\n+(50 by default). On observed `efbf91c5` it took **2h 8m** to trip. If one\\n+iteration takes 3 minutes to produce a no-op compaction event, 50 events =\\n+~150 min — matches the observed tail.\\n+\\n+**Impact:** on its own, the breaker is too slow to be a useful hang-guard. It\\n+should be replaced or complemented by a wall-clock-bounded variant.\\n+\\n+### RC4 — Provider HTTP requests likely lack a per-request deadline\\n+\\n+`RunAgent` passes the internal `context.Background()`-derived ctx into\\n+`agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`. The agent library's providers (`openai.Client`,\\n+anthropic client) do not appear to install a per-request deadline themselves\\n+(grepping the v0.3.14 module cache shows short timeouts on discovery/probe\\n+only; the main call path has no WithTimeout). A stalled TCP socket after\\n+headers are received but before the response body arrives can hang a streaming\\n+read indefinitely.\\n+\\n+**Impact:** consistent with \\\"alive but mid-LLM-call with no client-side\\n+deadline\\\" — hypothesis (2) in the bead description. Cannot be fully confirmed\\n+from source alone; needs runtime `go tool pprof http://localhost:<port>/debug/pprof/goroutine`\\n+against a live hung worker.\\n+\\n+### RC-skipped — BashTool subprocess hang (hypothesis 1)\\n+\\n+Not the root cause. `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14/tool/bash.go:15`\\n+installs a 120s default wall-clock timeout (`context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)`)\\n+and calls `exec.CommandContext`. Per-call bash hangs self-terminate at 2min.\\n+\\n+### RC-skipped — Tracker-side claim leak (hypothesis 3)\\n+\\n+Tracker state is incidental to the hang: the bead's own description notes the\\n+hung workers exist as processes (`ps aux | grep claude`). The worker process is\\n+alive; the claim is alive because the worker is alive. Fixing the claim alone\\n+does not kill the worker. This hypothesis is downstream, not upstream.\\n+\\n+### RC-skipped — Worktree file-lock (hypothesis 4)\\n+\\n+No evidence in bundles. Execute-bead worktrees are plain git worktrees at\\n+`/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/*`; no process-held file locks would survive across 33–142h\\n+without the owning process. If the process is alive, the lock is alive — same\\n+as RC3.\\n+\\n+---\\n+\\n+## 3. Relationship to ddx-b808df39\\n+\\n+ddx-b808df39 proposes an operator `ddx agent workers stop <id>` command that\\n+sends **SIGTERM at the OS level**, then SIGKILL after a grace period. That\\n+design **does** work around RC1 (process-level signal bypasses the broken ctx\\n+plumbing). So ddx-b808df39 is a *correct mitigation* — but only a mitigation.\\n+\\n+Without fixing RC1 (and at least RC2), operators will keep needing to invoke\\n+`workers stop` manually. The autonomous remediations every other system relies\\n+on (cancel ctx, idle timeout, circuit breaker) are each broken today in ways\\n+that require explicit code changes, not just a new operator tool.\\n+\\n+**Recommendation:** Ship ddx-b808df39 first (quick operational relief), then\\n+resolve the child beads filed below in order RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3.\\n+\\n+---\\n+\\n+## 4. Follow-up beads filed\\n+\\n+Each addresses one root cause with a deterministic acceptance test. Priority\\n+and ID are shown; full details available via `ddx bead show <id>`.\\n+\\n+The IDs below carry an `.execute-bead-wt-…` prefix because `ddx bead create`\\n+was invoked from inside the isolated execute-bead worktree — this is a\\n+separate DDx bug worth noting but orthogonal to the hang investigation. They\\n+are parented to `ddx-0a651925` and resolvable by `ddx bead list --parent ddx-0a651925`.\\n+\\n+| RC  | Priority | Bead ID (suffix)                                                           | Title                                                              |\\n+| --- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |\\n+| RC1 | P0       | `…-526efaf1` | propagate caller context through Runner.Run and RunAgent           |\\n+| RC2 | P0       | `…-b203c889` | add wall-clock deadline alongside idle timeout for agent harness   |\\n+| RC3 | P2       | `…-b0562380` | bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock time                  |\\n+| RC4 | P1       | `…-3e3913a0` | audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent provider calls |\\n+| —   | P1       | `…-34d039b7` | autonomous worker watchdog with process-level reaper (complements ddx-b808df39) |\\n+\\n+**Suggested execution order:** RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3 → watchdog. RC1 is a\\n+prerequisite for RC4 being meaningful.\\n+\\n+---\\n+\\n+## 5. What this investigation could NOT do\\n+\\n+- Capture live `ps`/`pprof` from a hung worker — none was accessible from the\\n+  isolated execute-bead worktree. RC4 in particular needs a live `go tool pprof`\\n+  goroutine dump to confirm that the stuck goroutine is in `net/http` streaming\\n+  read, not some other path.\\n+- Confirm whether the agent library's providers install any timeout that was\\n+  missed in grep. Observed evidence (from `.ddx/executions/`)\\n+\\n+| Metric                                               | Count / value                                        |\\n+| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |\\n+| Total attempt bundles                                | 328                                                  |\\n+| Bundles with `result.json`                           | 291 (89%)                                            |\\n+| Bundles with `manifest.json` but **no** `result.json` | 37 (11%) — executions whose worker never finalized  |\\n+\\n+Top five longest *finalized* attempts:\\n+\\n+| `duration_ms` | Wall time    | `detail`                                                                    | Bundle                          |\\n+| ------------- | ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |\\n+| 7,720,908     | **2h 8m**    | `agent: compaction stuck: 50 consecutive failed compaction attempts`        | 20260416T230316-efbf91c5        |\\n+| 7,200,098     | **2h 0m**    | `timeout after 2h0m0s` (claude streaming wall-clock fired)                  | 20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba        |\\n+| 6,764,927     | **1h 53m**   | success (agent harness, qwen)                                               | 20260416T230315-f9f46822        |\\n+| 5,649,987     | **1h 34m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260416T230313-e193942b        |\\n+| 4,284,347     | **1h 11m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260415T103422-bb3a45fa        |\\n+\\n+**Key observation:** the 33h / 142h hang durations cited in ddx-b808df39 are\\n+~15× and ~65× longer than the longest *finalized* attempt on record. Root-cause classification (source code evidence)\\n+\\n+The investigation checked the four hypotheses from the bead description.\\n+\\n+### RC1 (PRIMARY) — Caller context is discarded inside the Runner\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (`Runner.Run`):\\n+```go\\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\\n+```\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (`Runner.RunAgent`):\\n+```go\\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\\n+```\\n+\\n+`RunOptions` (types.go) takes no `context.Context` at all. `agent.ExecuteBead`\\n+likewise does not accept ctx, and `server.WorkerManager.runWorker` passes its\\n+own ctx into `worker.Run(ctx, …)` but that ctx **never reaches the agent\\n+invocation**.\\n+\\n+**Impact:** `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (workers.go:648) calls `handle.cancel()`\\n+which cancels only the worker goroutine's ctx. The running agent provider call\\n+/ subprocess is detached. The only way to interrupt a stuck agent today is a\\n+SIGKILL from outside the process. This is the structural defect that enables\\n+33–142h hangs — once the agent is stuck, nothing inside DDx can stop it.\\n+\\n+### RC2 — Idle-reset timer, not wall-clock deadline\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/executor.go:35-40` creates an *idle timeout* context value:\\n+```go\\n+func withExecutionTimeout(ctx context.Context, timeout time.Duration) context.Context {\\n+    return context.WithValue(ctx, executionTimeoutKey{}, timeout)\\n+}\\n+```\\n+\\n+`executor.go:154-178` consumes it and **resets the timer on every stdout/stderr\\n+byte**. `agent_runner.go:264-285` implements the same pattern against agentlib\\n+events.\\n+\\n+The default timeout is 2h (`types.go:363 DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000`). The\\n+comment claims \\\"2 hours — long enough for any task; supervisor/agent decides\\n+termination\\\" but the actual behaviour is **\\\"2 hours of silence required to fire\\\"**.\\n+A provider that emits a heartbeat, a ping, a retry line, or any byte resets the\\n+timer indefinitely.\\n+\\n+The `runClaudeStreaming` path (`claude_stream.go:373-384`) is the only path that\\n+uses a wall-clock deadline — and it is the only path we see actually hitting\\n+the timeout (bundle 20260415T015231 exactly 2h 0m 0.098s).\\n+\\n+**Impact:** for `harness=agent` (qwen, minimax, openrouter, etc.), no wall-clock\\n+bound exists. 33–142h hangs are consistent with a provider that dribbles events\\n+slowly enough to defeat the idle reset.\\n+\\n+### RC3 — Compaction-stuck breaker fires late\\n+\\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` increments a counter on each no-op\\n+compaction event and trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures`\\n+(50 by default). On observed `efbf91c5` it took **2h 8m** to trip. If one\\n+iteration takes 3 minutes to produce a no-op compaction event, 50 events =\\n+~150 min — matches the observed tail.\\n+\\n+**Impact:** on its own, the breaker is too slow to be a useful hang-guard. It\\n+should be replaced or complemented by a wall-clock-bounded variant.\\n+\\n+### RC4 — Provider HTTP requests likely lack a per-request deadline\\n+\\n+`RunAgent` passes the internal `context.Background()`-derived ctx into\\n+`agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`. The agent library's providers (`openai.Client`,\\n+anthropic client) do not appear to install a per-request deadline themselves\\n+(grepping the v0.3.14 module cache shows short timeouts on discovery/probe\\n+only; the main call path has no WithTimeout). A stalled TCP socket after\\n+headers are received but before the response body arrives can hang a streaming\\n+read indefinitely.\\n+\\n+**Impact:** consistent with \\\"alive but mid-LLM-call with no client-side\\n+deadline\\\" — hypothesis (2) in the bead description. Cannot be fully confirmed\\n+from source alone; needs runtime `go tool pprof http://localhost:<port>/debug/pprof/goroutine`\\n+against a live hung worker.\\n+\\n+### RC-skipped — BashTool subprocess hang (hypothesis 1)\\n+\\n+Not the root cause. `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14/tool/bash.go:15`\\n+installs a 120s default wall-clock timeout (`context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)`)\\n+and calls `exec.CommandContext`. Per-call bash hangs self-terminate at 2min.\\n+\\n+### RC-skipped — Tracker-side claim leak (hypothesis 3)\\n+\\n+Tracker state is incidental to the hang: the bead's own description notes the\\n+hung workers exist as processes (`ps aux | grep claude`). The worker process is\\n+alive; the claim is alive because the worker is alive. Fixing the claim alone\\n+does not kill the worker. This hypothesis is downstream, not upstream.\\n+\\n+### RC-skipped — Worktree file-lock (hypothesis 4)\\n+\\n+No evidence in bundles. Execute-bead worktrees are plain git worktrees at\\n+`/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/*`; no process-held file locks would survive across 33–142h\\n+without the owning process. If the process is alive, the lock is alive — same\\n+as RC3.\\n+\\n+---\\n+\\n+## 3. Relationship to ddx-b808df39\\n+\\n+ddx-b808df39 proposes an operator `ddx agent workers stop <id>` command that\\n+sends **SIGTERM at the OS level**, then SIGKILL after a grace period. That\\n+design **does** work around RC1 (process-level signal bypasses the broken ctx\\n+plumbing). So ddx-b808df39 is a *correct mitigation* — but only a mitigation.\\n+\\n+Without fixing RC1 (and at least RC2), operators will keep needing to invoke\\n+`workers stop` manually. The autonomous remediations every other system relies\\n+on (cancel ctx, idle timeout, circuit breaker) are each broken today in ways\\n+that require explicit code changes, not just a new operator tool.\\n+\\n+**Recommendation:** Ship ddx-b808df39 first (quick operational relief), then\\n+resolve the child beads filed below in order RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3.\\n+\\n+---\\n+\\n+## 4. The epic\ncannot legitimately close until those close, and the prior review correctly\nreturned REVIEW:BLOCK on a previous attempt that produced an empty result.json\nwithout advancing any child.\n\nThe right thing for the queue to do is execute the children (in dependency\norder: 16.0 -> 16.1 -> 16.2 -> 16.3), then re-queue this epic so the closure\ngate flips green. No code change is appropriate here; doing implementation\nwork under the epic ID would land changes that should belong to a child bead's\nattempt and confuse traceability.\n\nWhat is done:\n  - Decomposition is already recorded in the parent's notes (see bead notes).\n  - All four children exist with focused AC and the correct dependency edges.\n\nWhat is blocking:\n  - Children ddx-6d132f62, ddx-f04faa86, ddx-4cd64068, ddx-70a432b5 are open.\n\nWhat a follow-up attempt needs:\n  - Run the children to completion in dependency order, then re-attempt this\n    epic. .ddx/executions/20260504T021759-c712d0e8/manifest.json:13:    "description": "The bug at ddx-29058e2a (5 CLI flags silently dropped on the server path) is a symptom of a deeper architectural issue: the ExecuteLoopSpec is defined as parallel structs that must be hand-maintained in lockstep. Adding any flag requires editing 5+ places, and forgetting one is silent.\n\nCURRENT STATE (the smell)\n\nEach flag traverses 6 boundaries:\n\n1. cobra extract: cmd.Flags().GetX(\"flag\") → local var (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1573-1611)\n2. positional param: passed to executeLoopWithServer / runExecuteLoopLocal (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1627, ~1941 signature)\n3. loose JSON map: workerSpec map[string]interface{} (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:~1948)\n4. server request struct: hand-maintained in handleStartExecuteLoopWorker (cli/internal/server/server.go:2246-2260)\n5. ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec literal: server.go:2289-2303 → cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49\n6. runWorker per-field consumption: cli/internal/server/workers.go ~656\n\nALSO IN SCOPE — the GraphQL parallel path (per codex review):\n- workerDispatchAdapter at cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:62-75, 123-137 has its own request struct AND its own StartExecuteLoop literal — same parallel-struct smell on a different transport. Unification must DELETE this too, otherwise the refactor fixes REST and leaves GraphQL re-broken.\n\nTwo of those (steps 4 and 5) are typed but maintained in parallel. The 5 dropped flags (opaque_passthrough, max_cost, request_timeout, min_power, max_power) fell out exactly there. PLUS FromRev is also dropped on the server path (cobra flag at agent_cmd.go:1560 + work.go:58, consumed locally at agent_cmd.go:1753 ExecuteBeadRuntime{FromRev: fromRev}, never reaches the server). The bug fix would be local — add fields in 5 places — but the ROOT CAUSE recurs the next time a flag is added.\n\nDESIGN — single canonical ExecuteLoopSpec struct\n\nDefine one struct, used at every layer:\n\n  cobra RunE → parseExecuteLoopSpec(cmd) (ExecuteLoopSpec, DispatchOptions)\n                ↓\n       (--local) runExecuteLoopInline(spec)\n                ↓\n       (default) httpSubmitExecuteLoop(spec)  ── JSON ──▶ server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker\n                                                   ↓ (decode INTO THE SAME struct, then Validate + ApplyDefaults)\n                                                startExecuteLoop(spec)\n                                                   ↓ (persist as spec.json — SAME struct, fully-resolved)\n                                                runWorker(spec)\n\nProperties:\n- One struct definition. JSON tags drive both wire format and disk persistence.\n- Cobra binding is the only per-flag code (irreducible — cobra needs to know each flag).\n- --local and server paths consume the same struct; behavior parity is structural, not asserted by tests.\n- Adding a flag = (a) one struct field + (b) one cobra binding. Drop is impossible by construction — the JSON tag round-trips through wire and persistence.\n- DispatchOptions is a SEPARATE small struct holding control-plane flags (--local, --json) that are NOT execution-spec fields and must NOT be persisted on disk.\n\nCRITICAL CONTRACTS (per codex review)\n\n1. Wire format for time.Duration: Go's default time.Duration JSON marshaling produces nanoseconds, but current code sends poll_interval as a string (agent_cmd.go:1969-1971). The unified spec MUST commit to one format — either custom Duration type with string MarshalJSON, or canonical numeric. Pick string (operator-readable spec.json wins) and add UnmarshalJSON tolerating both.\n\n2. Validate + ApplyDefaults lifecycle:\n   - parseExecuteLoopSpec returns spec + DispatchOptions, with cobra defaults applied\n   - Spec.Validate() is called BEFORE HTTP submit (client-side guard)\n   - server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker decodes → calls Spec.ApplyDefaults() → Spec.Validate() BEFORE StartExecuteLoop (server cannot trust the wire)\n   - GraphQL workerDispatchAdapter goes through THE SAME StartExecuteLoop entry point so Validate + ApplyDefaults are guaranteed\n   - spec.json on disk is fully-resolved (no implicit defaults at runWorker time)\n\n3. Forward-compat stance: ddx is single-repo, single-version client/server today. Add SpecVersion int field; server returns 400 with capability list if SpecVersion \u003e supported. Newer-server-older-client: existing fields unchanged, new fields ignored on both sides (the current default). Make this explicit in the AC; do not rely on json's silent unknown-field tolerance as the only contract.\n\n4. Backward-compat for old persisted specs: existing spec.json files in .ddx/workers/ have legacy fields (e.g., min_tier/max_tier in older specs at .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T083934-f77c/spec.json:5-6). UnmarshalJSON must tolerate unknown fields when LOADING from disk (json.Decoder default behavior is fine here; do NOT enable DisallowUnknownFields for disk reads).\n\nLOCATION\n\nLikely cli/internal/agent/spec.go or cli/internal/agent/executeloop/spec.go (a small focused package). Importable from both cli/cmd/ and cli/internal/server/ without import cycles. NOTE: REACH-PROTO (ddx-a946c744) needs to know about this new package as an entry-root for the production-reachability checker.\n\nNOT IN SCOPE\n\n- Other commands (ddx agent run, ddx try, ddx run, MCP agent dispatch, ddx work passthrough variants) — same smell may exist; covered by the sister audit bead ddx-fb290074.\n- Reworking client→server transport (HTTP/JSON stays as-is).\n- Changing validation logic itself — only ensuring it lives on the spec so both paths apply it.\n- Renaming flags or reshaping CLI surface.\n- Reshaping LabelFilter from string to []string — that is a picker semantics change, owned by ddx-9d55601f. Keep LabelFilter as string to keep ddx-9d55601f landing in parallel.\n\nUSER-OBSERVED SYMPTOM (originally what filed this bead)\n- ddx work --harness claude works under --local but fails when going through the server.\n- Root cause: opaque_passthrough was dropped at the server's request struct. With OpaquePassthrough lost, the server-spawned worker validates the harness against its provider list (which rejects 'claude') instead of treating it as opaque-passthrough.\n- The unification refactor solves this by construction — there's no second struct to omit OpaquePassthrough from. NOTE: OpaquePassthrough has TWO skip points to verify (cli/internal/server/workers.go:314-317 AND :752-773), not one.\n\nABSORBS THE NARROW FIX\nThe 5 originally-listed dropped flags + FromRev all become fields on the unified spec. They cannot be dropped after this refactor. The refactor IS the fix.\n\nSEQUENCING WITH IN-FLIGHT BEADS\n\n- ddx-dc157075 (worker stay-alive): touches execute_bead_loop.go's Run loop. Should land BEFORE this OR be tightly coordinated. If unification lands first, dc157075 redoes defaulting + persistence. Recommend dc157075 first.\n- ddx-9d55601f (picker priority bug): does NOT block on this. Keep LabelFilter type stable. Picker fix lands in parallel.\n- ddx-a946c744 (REACH-PROTO): needs to know about new cli/internal/agent/executeloop/ package as entry root.",
.ddx/executions/20260504T021759-c712d0e8/manifest.json:591:          "body": "Architectural conflict with open bead ddx-eccc6efb (ADR-022 step 7), plus the bead materially exceeds the size-check thresholds. Human triage required to resolve the design conflict before any unification work commits. ## Summary The bead asks to UNIFY the parallel ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec / cobra-flag / HTTP-request structs into one canonical `ExecuteLoopSpec` struct used at every layer. But the open ADR-022 step 7 epic (ddx-eccc6efb, status=open) takes the opposite direction: DELETE `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec` entirely and replace the in-process server-spawn path with `exec ddx work` (subprocess), at which point there are no parallel structs because the server stops marshalling a spec at all — it just builds an argv vector. These are mutually-exclusive architectural directions for the same code. Landing this bead's unification would either: (a) be partially undone when ADR-022 step 7 deletes the unified struct, or (b) lock in the unified-struct design and require ADR-022 step 7 to be re-scoped or abandoned. The bead's \"SEQUENCING WITH IN-FLIGHT BEADS\" section names dc157075, 9d55601f, and a946c744, but is silent on ddx-eccc6efb / ADR-022 step 7. That gap is the load-bearing decision this bead does not make. ## Evidence 1. `cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49` — current `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec` exists with the fields the bead lists (and is missing MaxCostUSD/RequestTimeout/MinPower/MaxPower/FromRev as the bead claims). 2. ADR-022 step 7 (ddx-eccc6efb) is OPEN with acceptance: \"Legacy ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec serialization deletes when this lands.\" Confirmed via `ddx bead show ddx-eccc6efb`. 3. ADR-022 step 4 (ddx-85165814) already CLOSED → `--local` is a deprecated no-op (`cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1576-1577`). The cobra path no longer branches to server-submit; `executeLoopWithServer` (`cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1986`) is unreferenced (verified: only the function definition matches; no call sites). 4. Therefore the bead's USER-OBSERVED SYMPTOM (\"ddx work --harness claude works under --local but fails when going through the server\") cannot be reproduced via the CLI today — `--local` is a no-op and the server-submit branch from cobra is dead code. The remaining server-spawn path is via `POST /api/agent/workers/execute-loop` (`cli/internal/server/server.go:2390`) and the GraphQL `workerDispatchAdapter` (`cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:127`), both of which ADR-022 step 7 wants to delete in favor of subprocess exec. 5. Recent ddx-eccc6efb attempt (20260503T065047-61e4ed20, result.json) was DECLINED for size and decomposed into proposed children 7a/7b/7c — those children may not yet be filed in the main repo per its own bookkeeping note, but the intent stands. ## Size-check verdict (independent of the conflict) The bead also fails the size check on its own: - 17 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems (cobra flag binding, client-side validation, HTTP handler, GraphQL adapter, workers.go consumer, on-disk persistence, backward-compat for legacy fields, forward-compat with SpecVersion, custom Duration codec, reflection-driven test, manual end-to-end run). - Touches at minimum: new package `cli/internal/agent/executeloop/` (struct + Validate + ApplyDefaults + custom Duration + tests), `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go` (cobra rebind + dispatch split), `cli/internal/server/server.go` (handler rewrite + handler tests), `cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go` + `graphql_adapters_default_profile_test.go` + `workers_config_test.go` (parallel-construction deletion), `cli/internal/server/workers.go` (consumer rewrite), and a literal sweep across at least 8 test files (`workers_test.go`, `workers_e2e_test.go`, `workers_prune_test.go`, `workers_stop_test.go`, `workers_stop_propagation_test.go`, `workers_watchdog_test.go`, plus the two GraphQL test files) that all construct `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec{}` literals. - File-size signal: workers.go 1959 LOC, server.go 5248 LOC, agent_cmd.go 2245 LOC, workers_test.go 1107 LOC. Estimated change footprint comfortably \u003e500 LOC across \u003e5 files in unrelated packages. - Per execution-bead protocol size check: \"More than ~6 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems\" + \"More than ~500 lines across more than ~5 files in unrelated packages\" → DECOMPOSE. ## Why I am not decomposing in this attempt The protocol's decomposition path commits the project to a particular shape (in this case: unification rather than deletion). Filing 4–6 child beads under ddx-29058e2a would advance an architectural direction that is in unresolved conflict with ddx-eccc6efb. A clean decomposition only makes sense AFTER the parent-vs-step-7 design choice is made by a human: Option A — Drop ddx-29058e2a in favor of ADR-022 step 7. The parallel-struct smell goes away by deletion, not unification. The 5 currently-dropped flags can be patched as a narrow fix on the existing struct in the meantime if the operator workflow needs them, with a note that step 7 will erase the struct. Option B — Keep ddx-29058e2a and amend / close ddx-eccc6efb. The unified spec becomes the canonical wire format; ADR-022 step 7 reduces in scope (or is closed as superseded) because the server keeps marshalling a spec, just a unified one. Option C — Sequence: land the narrow 5-flag fix now (small bead), then land ADR-022 step 7 (which deletes the struct and obsoletes the parallel-struct smell), then close ddx-29058e2a as superseded. Each option has different downstream implications (ddx-fb290074 audit scope, REACH-PROTO entry roots, where Validate/ApplyDefaults live, whether SpecVersion is needed at all). I do not have authority to pick. ## Recommended next actions for the orchestrator / triage 1. Decide which of A / B / C above is the intended path. This is a 5-minute design call, not a research task. 2. If A: close ddx-29058e2a as superseded; optionally file a small fix bead \"add MaxCostUSD/RequestTimeout/MinPower/MaxPower/FromRev to ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec + propagate through handler\" (~50 LOC, will be deleted by step 7 anyway). 3. If B: amend ddx-eccc6efb's scope (or close it), then decompose ddx-29058e2a into ~5 children: (i) new spec package + Validate/ApplyDefaults + Duration codec + reflection test, (ii) cobra rebind + parseExecuteLoopSpec + dispatch split, (iii) HTTP handler decode-into-spec rewrite + delete handler-local request struct, (iv) GraphQL adapter unification, (v) workers.go consumer migration + workers_*_test literal sweep + spec.json on-disk format flip + SpecVersion gate. 4. If C: file the narrow 5-flag fix as its own bead now; pause ddx-29058e2a until after step 7 lands, at which point most of its ACs disappear and what's left (if anything) can be re-scoped. ## What this attempt did NOT do (and why) - No code changes. The conflict is upstream of any implementation choice. - No child beads filed. Filing children would commit to the unification path; see \"Why I am not decomposing\" above. - No partial fix to the 5 dropped flags. The bead explicitly absorbs that narrow fix; doing it here without addressing the unification framing would muddy the bead's status.",

If C: file the narrow 5-flag fix as its own bead now; pause ddx-29058e2a until after step 7 lands, at which point most of its ACs disappear and what's left (if anything) can be re-scoped.\n\n## What this attempt did NOT do (and why)\n\n- No code changes. .ddx/executions/20260504T021759-c712d0e8/prompt.md:5:The bug at ddx-29058e2a (5 CLI flags silently dropped on the server path) is a symptom of a deeper architectural issue: the ExecuteLoopSpec is defined as parallel structs that must be hand-maintained in lockstep. Adding any flag requires editing 5+ places, and forgetting one is silent.

CURRENT STATE (the smell)

Each flag traverses 6 boundaries:

1. cobra extract: cmd.Flags().GetX("flag") → local var (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1573-1611)
2. positional param: passed to executeLoopWithServer / runExecuteLoopLocal (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1627, ~1941 signature)
3. loose JSON map: workerSpec map[string]interface{} (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:~1948)
4. server request struct: hand-maintained in handleStartExecuteLoopWorker (cli/internal/server/server.go:2246-2260)
5. ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec literal: server.go:2289-2303 → cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49
6. runWorker per-field consumption: cli/internal/server/workers.go ~656

ALSO IN SCOPE — the GraphQL parallel path (per codex review):
- workerDispatchAdapter at cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:62-75, 123-137 has its own request struct AND its own StartExecuteLoop literal — same parallel-struct smell on a different transport. Unification must DELETE this too, otherwise the refactor fixes REST and leaves GraphQL re-broken.

Two of those (steps 4 and 5) are typed but maintained in parallel. The 5 dropped flags (opaque_passthrough, max_cost, request_timeout, min_power, max_power) fell out exactly there. PLUS FromRev is also dropped on the server path (cobra flag at agent_cmd.go:1560 + work.go:58, consumed locally at agent_cmd.go:1753 ExecuteBeadRuntime{FromRev: fromRev}, never reaches the server). The bug fix would be local — add fields in 5 places — but the ROOT CAUSE recurs the next time a flag is added.

DESIGN — single canonical ExecuteLoopSpec struct

Define one struct, used at every layer:

  cobra RunE → parseExecuteLoopSpec(cmd) (ExecuteLoopSpec, DispatchOptions)
                ↓
       (--local) runExecuteLoopInline(spec)
                ↓
       (default) httpSubmitExecuteLoop(spec)  ── JSON ──▶ server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker
                                                   ↓ (decode INTO THE SAME struct, then Validate + ApplyDefaults)
                                                startExecuteLoop(spec)
                                                   ↓ (persist as spec.json — SAME struct, fully-resolved)
                                                runWorker(spec)

Properties:
- One struct definition. JSON tags drive both wire format and disk persistence.
- Cobra binding is the only per-flag code (irreducible — cobra needs to know each flag).
- --local and server paths consume the same struct; behavior parity is structural, not asserted by tests.
- Adding a flag = (a) one struct field + (b) one cobra binding. Drop is impossible by construction — the JSON tag round-trips through wire and persistence.
- DispatchOptions is a SEPARATE small struct holding control-plane flags (--local, --json) that are NOT execution-spec fields and must NOT be persisted on disk.

CRITICAL CONTRACTS (per codex review)

1. Wire format for time.Duration: Go's default time.Duration JSON marshaling produces nanoseconds, but current code sends poll_interval as a string (agent_cmd.go:1969-1971). The unified spec MUST commit to one format — either custom Duration type with string MarshalJSON, or canonical numeric. Pick string (operator-readable spec.json wins) and add UnmarshalJSON tolerating both.

2. Validate + ApplyDefaults lifecycle:
   - parseExecuteLoopSpec returns spec + DispatchOptions, with cobra defaults applied
   - Spec.Validate() is called BEFORE HTTP submit (client-side guard)
   - server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker decodes → calls Spec.ApplyDefaults() → Spec.Validate() BEFORE StartExecuteLoop (server cannot trust the wire)
   - GraphQL workerDispatchAdapter goes through THE SAME StartExecuteLoop entry point so Validate + ApplyDefaults are guaranteed
   - spec.json on disk is fully-resolved (no implicit defaults at runWorker time)

3. Forward-compat stance: ddx is single-repo, single-version client/server today. Add SpecVersion int field; server returns 400 with capability list if SpecVersion > supported. Newer-server-older-client: existing fields unchanged, new fields ignored on both sides (the current default). Make this explicit in the AC; do not rely on json's silent unknown-field tolerance as the only contract.

4. Backward-compat for old persisted specs: existing spec.json files in .ddx/workers/ have legacy fields (e.g., min_tier/max_tier in older specs at .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T083934-f77c/spec.json:5-6). UnmarshalJSON must tolerate unknown fields when LOADING from disk (json.Decoder default behavior is fine here; do NOT enable DisallowUnknownFields for disk reads).

LOCATION

Likely cli/internal/agent/spec.go or cli/internal/agent/executeloop/spec.go (a small focused package). Importable from both cli/cmd/ and cli/internal/server/ without import cycles. NOTE: REACH-PROTO (ddx-a946c744) needs to know about this new package as an entry-root for the production-reachability checker.

NOT IN SCOPE

- Other commands (ddx agent run, ddx try, ddx run, MCP agent dispatch, ddx work passthrough variants) — same smell may exist; covered by the sister audit bead ddx-fb290074.
- Reworking client→server transport (HTTP/JSON stays as-is).
- Changing validation logic itself — only ensuring it lives on the spec so both paths apply it.
- Renaming flags or reshaping CLI surface.
- Reshaping LabelFilter from string to []string — that is a picker semantics change, owned by ddx-9d55601f. Keep LabelFilter as string to keep ddx-9d55601f landing in parallel.

USER-OBSERVED SYMPTOM (originally what filed this bead)
- ddx work --harness claude works under --local but fails when going through the server.
- Root cause: opaque_passthrough was dropped at the server's request struct. With OpaquePassthrough lost, the server-spawned worker validates the harness against its provider list (which rejects 'claude') instead of treating it as opaque-passthrough.
- The unification refactor solves this by construction — there's no second struct to omit OpaquePassthrough from. NOTE: OpaquePassthrough has TWO skip points to verify (cli/internal/server/workers.go:314-317 AND :752-773), not one.

ABSORBS THE NARROW FIX
The 5 originally-listed dropped flags + FromRev all become fields on the unified spec. They cannot be dropped after this refactor. The refactor IS the fix.

SEQUENCING WITH IN-FLIGHT BEADS

- ddx-dc157075 (worker stay-alive): touches execute_bead_loop.go's Run loop. Should land BEFORE this OR be tightly coordinated. If unification lands first, dc157075 redoes defaulting + persistence. Recommend dc157075 first.
- ddx-9d55601f (picker priority bug): does NOT block on this. Keep LabelFilter type stable. Picker fix lands in parallel.
- ddx-a946c744 (REACH-PROTO): needs to know about new cli/internal/agent/executeloop/ package as entry root.

.ddx/executions/20260504T022245-36a2ca7b/prompt.md:742:+          "body": "Architectural conflict with open bead ddx-eccc6efb (ADR-022 step 7), plus the bead materially exceeds the size-check thresholds. Human triage required to resolve the design conflict before any unification work commits. ## Summary The bead asks to UNIFY the parallel ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec / cobra-flag / HTTP-request structs into one canonical `ExecuteLoopSpec` struct used at every layer. But the open ADR-022 step 7 epic (ddx-eccc6efb, status=open) takes the opposite direction: DELETE `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec` entirely and replace the in-process server-spawn path with `exec ddx work` (subprocess), at which point there are no parallel structs because the server stops marshalling a spec at all — it just builds an argv vector. These are mutually-exclusive architectural directions for the same code. Landing this bead's unification would either: (a) be partially undone when ADR-022 step 7 deletes the unified struct, or (b) lock in the unified-struct design and require ADR-022 step 7 to be re-scoped or abandoned. The bead's \"SEQUENCING WITH IN-FLIGHT BEADS\" section names dc157075, 9d55601f, and a946c744, but is silent on ddx-eccc6efb / ADR-022 step 7. That gap is the load-bearing decision this bead does not make. ## Evidence 1. `cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49` — current `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec` exists with the fields the bead lists (and is missing MaxCostUSD/RequestTimeout/MinPower/MaxPower/FromRev as the bead claims). 2. ADR-022 step 7 (ddx-eccc6efb) is OPEN with acceptance: \"Legacy ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec serialization deletes when this lands.\" Confirmed via `ddx bead show ddx-eccc6efb`. 3. ADR-022 step 4 (ddx-85165814) already CLOSED → `--local` is a deprecated no-op (`cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1576-1577`). The cobra path no longer branches to server-submit; `executeLoopWithServer` (`cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1986`) is unreferenced (verified: only the function definition matches; no call sites). 4. Therefore the bead's USER-OBSERVED SYMPTOM (\"ddx work --harness claude works under --local but fails when going through the server\") cannot be reproduced via the CLI today — `--local` is a no-op and the server-submit branch from cobra is dead code. The remaining server-spawn path is via `POST /api/agent/workers/execute-loop` (`cli/internal/server/server.go:2390`) and the GraphQL `workerDispatchAdapter` (`cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:127`), both of which ADR-022 step 7 wants to delete in favor of subprocess exec. 5. Recent ddx-eccc6efb attempt (20260503T065047-61e4ed20, result.json) was DECLINED for size and decomposed into proposed children 7a/7b/7c — those children may not yet be filed in the main repo per its own bookkeeping note, but the intent stands. ## Size-check verdict (independent of the conflict) The bead also fails the size check on its own: - 17 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems (cobra flag binding, client-side validation, HTTP handler, GraphQL adapter, workers.go consumer, on-disk persistence, backward-compat for legacy fields, forward-compat with SpecVersion, custom Duration codec, reflection-driven test, manual end-to-end run). - Touches at minimum: new package `cli/internal/agent/executeloop/` (struct + Validate + ApplyDefaults + custom Duration + tests), `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go` (cobra rebind + dispatch split), `cli/internal/server/server.go` (handler rewrite + handler tests), `cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go` + `graphql_adapters_default_profile_test.go` + `workers_config_test.go` (parallel-construction deletion), `cli/internal/server/workers.go` (consumer rewrite), and a literal sweep across at least 8 test files (`workers_test.go`, `workers_e2e_test.go`, `workers_prune_test.go`, `workers_stop_test.go`, `workers_stop_propagation_test.go`, `workers_watchdog_test.go`, plus the two GraphQL test files) that all construct `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec{}` literals. - File-size signal: workers.go 1959 LOC, server.go 5248 LOC, agent_cmd.go 2245 LOC, workers_test.go 1107 LOC. Estimated change footprint comfortably \u003e500 LOC across \u003e5 files in unrelated packages. - Per execution-bead protocol size check: \"More than ~6 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems\" + \"More than ~500 lines across more than ~5 files in unrelated packages\" → DECOMPOSE. ## Why I am not decomposing in this attempt The protocol's decomposition path commits the project to a particular shape (in this case: unification rather than deletion). Filing 4–6 child beads under ddx-29058e2a would advance an architectural direction that is in unresolved conflict with ddx-eccc6efb. A clean decomposition only makes sense AFTER the parent-vs-step-7 design choice is made by a human: Option A — Drop ddx-29058e2a in favor of ADR-022 step 7. The parallel-struct smell goes away by deletion, not unification. The 5 currently-dropped flags can be patched as a narrow fix on the existing struct in the meantime if the operator workflow needs them, with a note that step 7 will erase the struct. Option B — Keep ddx-29058e2a and amend / close ddx-eccc6efb. The unified spec becomes the canonical wire format; ADR-022 step 7 reduces in scope (or is closed as superseded) because the server keeps marshalling a spec, just a unified one. Option C — Sequence: land the narrow 5-flag fix now (small bead), then land ADR-022 step 7 (which deletes the struct and obsoletes the parallel-struct smell), then close ddx-29058e2a as superseded. Each option has different downstream implications (ddx-fb290074 audit scope, REACH-PROTO entry roots, where Validate/ApplyDefaults live, whether SpecVersion is needed at all). I do not have authority to pick. ## Recommended next actions for the orchestrator / triage 1. Decide which of A / B / C above is the intended path. This is a 5-minute design call, not a research task. 2. If A: close ddx-29058e2a as superseded; optionally file a small fix bead \"add MaxCostUSD/RequestTimeout/MinPower/MaxPower/FromRev to ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec + propagate through handler\" (~50 LOC, will be deleted by step 7 anyway). 3. If B: amend ddx-eccc6efb's scope (or close it), then decompose ddx-29058e2a into ~5 children: (i) new spec package + Validate/ApplyDefaults + Duration codec + reflection test, (ii) cobra rebind + parseExecuteLoopSpec + dispatch split, (iii) HTTP handler decode-into-spec rewrite + delete handler-local request struct, (iv) GraphQL adapter unification, (v) workers.go consumer migration + workers_*_test literal sweep + spec.json on-disk format flip + SpecVersion gate. 4. If C: file the narrow 5-flag fix as its own bead now; pause ddx-29058e2a until after step 7 lands, at which point most of its ACs disappear and what's left (if anything) can be re-scoped. ## What this attempt did NOT do (and why) - No code changes. The conflict is upstream of any implementation choice. - No child beads filed. Filing children would commit to the unification path; see \"Why I am not decomposing\" above. - No partial fix to the 5 dropped flags. The bead explicitly absorbs that narrow fix; doing it here without addressing the unification framing would muddy the bead's status.",

.ddx/executions/20260503T195715-4725673a/prompt.md:5:Per principle P7 (RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES bead): a bead's description + AC must be sufficient context for a competent sub-agent to execute without hand-curation. Today many beads punt the investigation, omit concrete test names, and leave non-scope implicit. Hand-curated sub-agent prompts have been compensating; this bead surfaces the gap and produces a template.

ROOT CAUSE
Beads were authored expecting the executor to investigate first. Sub-agents (and ddx try / ddx work workers) need investigation done UPFRONT to execute reliably. Without it, the executor either:
- Gives up (no_changes after limited investigation), OR
- Produces wrong work (wrong file, wrong scope), OR
- Asks for clarification (not possible in autonomous drain)

ROOT CAUSE 2
No formal authoring template exists. CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md describe bead types but not bead structure or quality bar. New beads inherit whatever the operator was thinking at the moment.

SCOPE
1. Audit 20 representative open beads (mix of priorities, areas, types) against a quality rubric.
2. Identify common gaps; quantify (how many beads miss test names, how many lack file:line cites, etc.).
3. Produce bead-authoring-template.md at docs/helix/06-iterate/.
4. Update CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and DDx skill at .agents/skills/ddx/ to reference the template.
5. Identify which 10-15 high-impact open beads warrant retrofit (the next round of execution work depends on them).

QUALITY RUBRIC (the audit measures each bead against)
- (a) Title: one-line scope clarity
- (b) Description: problem + ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE + proposed fix + non-scope
- (c) AC #1-N: numbered, verifiable, includes specific test names
- (d) AC includes 'wired-in' assertion when introducing new code paths
- (e) AC includes go test command + lefthook gate
- (f) Labels: phase + area + kind + cross-refs (adr/spec/prevention)
- (g) Parent + Deps: explicit
- (h) Description reads as sufficient sub-agent prompt (no operator hand-curation needed)

Score per bead: 8/8 = passes; less = needs retrofit.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Retrofitting beads (separate follow-up beads filed from the audit's recommendations)
- Auto-validating bead structure on creation (future tooling bead)
- Reorganizing existing bead labels or hierarchies

INTERSECTIONS
- RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES bead (this audit operationalizes P7)
- Retrofit recommendations from this audit gate the dispatch of refactor children C4-C13 (bead retrofit MUST land before sub-agent dispatch with verbatim prompts)

2. The audited 20 beads are listed by ID with per-criterion pass/fail and a 'needs retrofit' verdict.
3. Aggregate stats: % of beads passing each criterion; top 3 most-common gaps.
4. docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md exists with: template fields (title/description/AC structure), examples (one good bead, one weak bead with annotations), authoring checklist.
5. CLAUDE.md gains a 'When filing beads' section pointing at the template.
6. .agents/skills/ddx/ skill (and the embedded copies under .claude/skills/ddx/) updated to require template fields when creating beads.
7. Recommendations section in the audit report names 10-15 open beads warranting retrofit (with prioritization). These become the input for Wave 1 of the refactor execution plan.
8. Conventional commit ending [<this-bead-id>].
9. lefthook run pre-commit passes.

NO SUB-AGENT DISPATCH from this work — the audit is read-only research. The retrofit beads are filed by operator after reviewing the audit recommendations.
